Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.3  Normal Forms Section 7.3.3 Comparison of BCNF and 3NF Content: Of the two normal forms for relational database schemas, 3NF and BCNF there are advantages to 3NF in that we know that it is always possible to obtain a 3NF design without sacrificing losslessness or dependency preservation. Nevertheless, there are disadvantages to 3NF: We may have to use null values to represent some of the possible meaningful relationships among data items, and there is the problem of repetition of information. Our goals of database design with functional dependencies are: 1. BCNF. 2. Losslessness. 3. Dependency preservation. Since it is not always possible to satisfy all three, we may be forced to choose between BCNF and dependency preservation with 3NF. It is worth noting that SQL does not provide a way of specifying functional dependencies, except for the special  Page 319 case of declaring superkeys by using the primary key or unique constraints. It is possible, although a little complicated, to write assertions that enforce a functional dependency (see Practice Exercise 7.9); unfortunately, currently no database system supports the complex assertions that are required to enforce arbitrary functional dependencies, and the assertions would be expensive to test. Thus even if we had a dependency-preserving decomposition, if we use standard SQL we can test efficiently only those functional dependencies whose lefthand side is a key. Although testing functional dependencies may involve a join if the decomposition is not dependency preserving, if the database system supports materialized views, we could in principle reduce the cost by storing the join result as materialized view; however, this approach is feasible only if the database system supports primary key constraints or unique constraints on materialized views

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.3  Normal Forms Section 7.4.2 Closure of Attribute Sets Content: We say that an attribute B is functionally determined by α if α → β. T o test whether a set α is a superkey, we must devise an algorithm for computing the set of attributes functionally determined by α. One way of doing this is to compute F + , take all functional dependencies with a as the left-hand side, and take the union of the right-hand sides of all such dependencies. However, doing so can be expensive, since F + can be large. An efficient algorithm for computing the set of attributes functionally determined by a is useful not only for testing whether a is a superkey, but also for several other tasks, as we shall see later in this section.  Page 323 Let a be a set of attributes. We call the set of all attributes functionally determined by a under a set F of functional dependencies the closure of a under F ; we denote it by α + . Figure 7.8 shows an algorithm, written in pseudocode, to compute α + . The input is a set F of functional dependencies and the set α of attributes. The output is stored in the variable result . Figure 7.8 An algorithm to compute a+, the closure of a under F. To illustrate how the algorithm works, we shall use it to compute ( AG ) + with the functional dependencies defined in Section 7.4.1. We start with result = AG . The first time that we execute the repeat loop to test each functional dependency, we find that: - A → B causes us to include B in result. T o see this fact, we observe that A → B is in F , A ⊆ result (which is AG ), so result : = result ∪ B . - A → C causes result to become ABCG . - CG → H causes result to become ABCGH . - CG→ I causes result to become ABCGHI . The second time that we execute the repeat loop, no new attributes are added to result, and the algorithm terminates. Let us see why the algorithm of Figure 7.8 is correct. The first step is correct because α → α always holds (by the reflexivity rule). We claim that, for any subset β of result, α  Page 324 → β

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Section 7.2.1 Notational Conventions Content: In discussing algorithms for relational database design, we shall need to talk about arbitrary relations and their schema, rather than talking only about examples. Recalling our introduction to the relational model in Chapter 2, we summarize our notation here. - In general, we use Greek letters for sets of attributes (e.g.,α). We use an uppercase Roman letter to refer to a relation schema. We use the notation r(R ) to show that the schema R is for relation r . A relation schema is a set of attributes, but not all sets of attributes are schemas.  - When we use a lowercase Greek letter, we are referring to a set of attributes that may or may not be a schema. A Roman letter is used when we wish to indicate that the set of attributes is definitely a schema. - When a set of attributes is a superkey, we may denote it by K . A superkey pertains to a specific relation schema, so we use the terminology ' K is a superkey for R .' - We use a lowercase name for relations. In our examples, these names are intended to be realistic (e.g., instructor ), while in our definitions and algorithms, we use single letters, like r . - The notation r ( R ) thus refers to the relation r with schema R . When we write r ( R ), we thus refer both to the relation and its schema. - A relation, has a particular value at any given time; we refer to that as an instance and use the term 'instance of r .' When it is clear that we are talking about an instance, we may use simply the relation name (e.g., r ). For simplicity, we assume that attribute names have only one meaning within the database schema.

Superkeys are used to represent constraints that are satisfied by all relations in the database. They are typically associated with a specific relation schema and are used to ensure that the database satisfies all the constraints. Superkeys are useful for representing constraints that are not necessarily satisfied by all relations, as they allow for the database to be designed without sacrificing losslessness or dependency preservation.
            Functional dependencies, on the other hand, are used to represent constraints that are satisfied by all relations in the database. They are typically associated with a specific relation schema and are used to ensure that the database satisfies all the constraints. Functional dependencies are useful for representing constraints that are not necessarily satisfied by all relations, as they allow for the database to be designed without sacrificing losslessness or dependency preservation.
            In summary, superkeys are used to represent constraints that are satisfied by all relations, while functional dependencies are used to represent constraints that are satisfied by all relations.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Section 7.2.1 Notational Conventions Content: In discussing algorithms for relational database design, we shall need to talk about arbitrary relations and their schema, rather than talking only about examples. Recalling our introduction to the relational model in Chapter 2, we summarize our notation here. - In general, we use Greek letters for sets of attributes (e.g.,α). We use an uppercase Roman letter to refer to a relation schema. We use the notation r(R ) to show that the schema R is for relation r . For simplicity, we assume that attribute names have only one meaning within the database schema.

To compare and contrast the use of superkeys versus functional dependencies in representing real-world constraints, let's analyze their roles and differences in a relational database context:

### Superkeys
1. **Definition and Purpose**:
   - A superkey is a set of attributes that can uniquely identify a tuple in a relation.
   - Superkeys are used to ensure that a relation has a primary key, which is a minimal superkey (a superkey that cannot be reduced without losing uniqueness).

2. **Real-World Constraints**:
   - Superkeys are often used to capture constraints that are fundamental to the structure of the data, such as the uniqueness of identifiers (e.g., student ID, instructor ID).
   - They help in ensuring that the data is consistent and that no duplicate entries can exist.

3. **Example**:
   - In a university database, a superkey might be a combination of attributes like `StudentID` and `Name`, ensuring that each student is uniquely identified by their ID and name.

### Functional Dependencies (FDs)
1. **Definition and Purpose**:
   - A functional dependency is a constraint that states that if a set of attributes (left-hand side) is known, then the value of the right-hand side is uniquely determined.
   - FDs are used to capture dependencies between attributes that are not directly related by a superkey but are meaningful in the real-world context.

2. **Real-World Constraints**:
   - FDs are used to capture more nuanced relationships, such as:
     - A student and instructor having only one name.
     - Each instructor and student being associated with only one department.
     - Each department having only one budget and one building.
   - They help in preserving the integrity of the data by ensuring that certain attributes are always associated with specific values.

3. **Example**:
   - In the university database, a functional dependency might be `Department → Budget` and `Department → Building`, ensuring that each

There are usually a variety of constraints (rules) on the data in the real world. For example, some of the constraints that are expected to hold in a university database are: 1. Students and instructors are uniquely identified by their ID. 2. Each student and instructor has only one name. 3. Each instructor and student is (primarily) associated with only one department. 2 4. Each department has only one value for its budget, and only one associated build-ing. Page 309 An instance of a relation that satisfies all such real-world constraints is called a legal instance of the relation; a legal instance of a database is one where all the relation instances are legal instances.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.3  Normal Forms Section 7.3.3 Comparison of BCNF and 3NF Content: Of the two normal forms for relational database schemas, 3NF and BCNF there are advantages to 3NF in that we know that it is always possible to obtain a 3NF design without sacrificing losslessness or dependency preservation. Nevertheless, there are disadvantages to 3NF: We may have to use null values to represent some of the possible meaningful relationships among data items, and there is the problem of repetition of information. Our goals of database design with functional dependencies are: 1. BCNF. 2. Losslessness. 3. Dependency preservation. Since it is not always possible to satisfy all three, we may be forced to choose between BCNF and dependency preservation with 3NF. It is worth noting that SQL does not provide a way of specifying functional dependencies, except for the special  Page 319 case of declaring superkeys by using the primary key or unique constraints. It is possible, although a little complicated, to write assertions that enforce a functional dependency (see Practice Exercise 7.9); unfortunately, currently no database system supports the complex assertions that are required to enforce arbitrary functional dependencies, and the assertions would be expensive to test. Thus even if we had a dependency-preserving decomposition, if we use standard SQL we can test efficiently only those functional dependencies whose lefthand side is a key. Although testing functional dependencies may involve a join if the decomposition is not dependency preserving, if the database system supports materialized views, we could in principle reduce the cost by storing the join result as materialized view; however, this approach is feasible only if the database system supports primary key constraints or unique constraints on materialized views

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.3  Normal Forms Section 7.4.2 Closure of Attribute Sets Content: We say that an attribute B is functionally determined by α if α → β. T o test whether a set α is a superkey, we must devise an algorithm for computing the set of attributes functionally determined by α. One way of doing this is to compute F + , take all functional dependencies with a as the left-hand side, and take the union of the right-hand sides of all such dependencies. However, doing so can be expensive, since F + can be large. An efficient algorithm for computing the set of attributes functionally determined by a is useful not only for testing whether a is a superkey, but also for several other tasks, as we shall see later in this section.  Page 323 Let a be a set of attributes. We call the set of all attributes functionally determined by a under a set F of functional dependencies the closure of a under F ; we denote it by α + . Figure 7.8 shows an algorithm, written in pseudocode, to compute α + . The input is a set F of functional dependencies and the set α of attributes. The output is stored in the variable result . Figure 7.8 An algorithm to compute a+, the closure of a under F. To illustrate how the algorithm works, we shall use it to compute ( AG ) + with the functional dependencies defined in Section 7.4.1. We start with result = AG . The first time that we execute the repeat loop to test each functional dependency, we find that: - A → B causes us to include B in result. T o see this fact, we observe that A → B is in F , A ⊆ result (which is AG ), so result : = result ∪ B . - A → C causes result to become ABCG . - CG → H causes result to become ABCGH . - CG→ I causes result to become ABCGHI . The second time that we execute the repeat loop, no new attributes are added to result, and the algorithm terminates. Let us see why the algorithm of Figure 7.8 is correct. The first step is correct because α → α always holds (by the reflexivity rule). We claim that, for any subset β of result, α  Page 324 → β

chunks: Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.9  Database-Design Process Section 7.9.1 E-R Model and Normalization Content: When we define an E-R diagram carefully, identifying all entity sets correctly, the relation schemas generated from the E-R diagram should not need much further normalization. However, there can be functional dependencies among attributes of an entity set. For instance, suppose an instructor entity set had attributes dept\_name and dept\_address, and there is a functional dependency dept\_name→ dept\_address. We would then need to normalize the relation generated from instructor . Most examples of such dependencies arise out of poor E-R diagram design. In the preceding example, if we had designed the E-R diagram correctly, we would have created a department entity set with attribute dept\_address and a relationship set between instructor and department . Similarly, a relationship set involving more than two entity sets may result in a schema that may not be in a desirable normal form. Since most relationship sets are binary, such cases are relatively rare. (In fact, some E-R-diagram variants actually make it difficult or impossible to specify nonbinary relationship sets.) Functional dependencies can help us detect poor E-R design. If the generated relation schemas are not in desired normal form, the problem can be fixed in the E-R diagram. That is, normalization can be done formally as part of data modeling. Alternatively, normalization can be left to the designer's intuition during E-R modeling, and it can be done formally on the relation schemas generated from the E-R model. A careful reader will have noted that in order for us to illustrate a need for mul-tivalued dependencies and fourth normal form, we had to begin with schemas that were not derived from our E-R design. Indeed, the process of creating an E-R design tends to generate 4NF designs. If a multivalued dependency holds and is not implied by the corresponding functional dependency, it usually arises from one of the following sources:  - A many-to-many relationship set

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.6  Decomposition Using Multivalued Dependencies Content: Some relation schemas, even though they are in BCNF, do not seem to be sufficiently normalized, in the sense that they still suffer from the problem of repetition of information. Consider a variation of the university organization where an instructor may be associated with multiple departments, and we have a relation: inst ( ID , dept\_name, name, street, city ) The astute reader will recognize this schema as a nonBCNF schema because of the functional dependency ID → name, street, city and because ID is not a key for inst . addresses (say, a winter home and a summer home). Then, Further assume that an instructor may have several we no longer wish to enforce the functional dependency 'ID→ street, city', though, we still want to enforce ' ID → name' (i.e., the university is not dealing with instructors who operate under multiple aliases!). Following the BCNF decomposition algorithm, we obtain two schemas:  ``` r 1 ( ID , name ) r 2 ( ID , dept_name, street, city ) ``` Both of these are in BCNF (recall that an instructor can be associated with multiple departments and a department may have several instructors, and therefore, neither ' ID → dept\_name' nor 'dept\_name → ID' hold). Despite r 2 being in BCNF, there is redundancy. We repeat the address information of each residence of an instructor once for each department with which the instructor is associated. We could solve this problem by decomposing r 2 further into: but there is no constraint that leads us to do this. To deal with this problem, we must define a new form of constraint, called a multivalued dependency. As we did for functional dependencies, we shall use multivalued dependencies to define a normal form for relation schemas. This normal form, called fourth normal form (4NF), is more restrictive than BCNF. We shall see that every 4NF schema is also in BCNF but there are BCNF schemas that are not in 4NF.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Content: A database models a set of entities and relationships in the real world. There are usually a variety of constraints (rules) on the data in the real world. For example, some of the constraints that are expected to hold in a university database are: 1. Students and instructors are uniquely identified by their ID. 2. Each student and instructor has only one name. 3. Each instructor and student is (primarily) associated with only one department. 2 4. Each department has only one value for its budget, and only one associated build-ing. Page 309 An instance of a relation that satisfies all such real-world constraints is called a legal instance of the relation; a legal instance of a database is one where all the relation instances are legal instances.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Section 7.2.2 Keys and Functional Dependencies Content: .  Given that a set of functional dependencies F holds on a relation r ( R ), it may be possible to infer that certain other functional dependencies must also hold on the relation. For example, given a schema r(A, B, C ), if functional dependencies A → B and B → C hold on r , we can infer the functional dependency A → C must also hold on r . This is because, given any value of A , there can be only one corresponding value for B , and for that value of B , there can only be one corresponding value for C . We study in Section 7.4.1, how to make such inferences. We shall use the notation F + to denote the closure of the set F , that is, the set of all functional dependencies that can be inferred given the set F . F + contains all of the functional dependencies in F .

answer:
To ensure that each student has only one unique grade value associated with them, we can define a functional dependency that captures this constraint. Here's how we can apply the concept of functional dependencies:

1. **Identify the Key and the Attribute of Interest:**
   - The key for the relation schema `student_id, name` (assuming `student_id` uniquely identifies each student).
   - The attribute of interest is `grade`.

2. **Define the Functional Dependency:**
   - We need to ensure that for any given `student_id`, there is only one `grade`. This can be expressed as `student_id -> grade`.

3. **Verify Triviality:**
   - Check if the dependency `student_id -> grade` is trivial. A dependency is trivial if the right-hand side (RHS) is a subset of the left-hand side (LHS). In this case, `grade` is not a subset of `student_id`, so the dependency is not trivial.

4. **Check for Inference:**
   - Given the dependency `student_id -> grade`, we can infer other dependencies. For example, if we have a dependency `student_id -> course_name`, we can infer `student_id -> grade` because the grade is associated with the course name and the course name is associated with the student ID.

5. **Closure of Functional Dependencies:**
   - The closure of the set of functional dependencies `F = {student_id -> grade, student_id -> course_name}` is `F+ = {student_id -> grade, student_id -> course_name, course_name -> grade}`. This closure ensures that all dependencies can be inferred from the original set.

By defining `student_id -> grade` and ensuring that all dependencies can be inferred, we ensure that each student has only one unique grade value associated with them. This approach uses the concept of functional dependencies to enforce the constraint that a student can have only one grade for a given course.

Consider a variation of the university organization where an instructor may be associated with multiple departments, and we have a relation: inst ( ID , dept\_name, name, street, city ) The astute reader will recognize this schema as a nonBCNF schema because of the functional dependency ID → name, street, city and because ID is not a key for inst . addresses (say, a winter home and a summer home). Then, Further assume that an instructor may have several we no longer wish to enforce the functional dependency 'ID→ street, city', though, we still want to enforce ' ID → name' (i.e., the university is not dealing with instructors who operate under multiple aliases!). Following the BCNF decomposition algorithm, we obtain two schemas:  ``` r 1 ( ID , name ) r 2 ( ID , dept_name, street, city ) ``` Both of these are in BCNF (recall that an instructor can be associated with multiple departments and a department may have several instructors, and therefore, neither ' ID → dept\_name' nor 'dept\_name → ID' hold). Despite r 2 being in BCNF, there is redundancy. We repeat the address information of each residence of an instructor once for each department with which the instructor is associated. We could solve this problem by decomposing r 2 further into: but there is no constraint that leads us to do this. To deal with this problem, we must define a new form of constraint, called a multivalued dependency. As we did for functional dependencies, we shall use multivalued dependencies to define a normal form for relation schemas. This normal form, called fourth normal form (4NF), is more restrictive than BCNF. We shall see that every 4NF schema is also in BCNF but there are BCNF schemas that are not in 4NF.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Section 7.2.2 Keys and Functional Dependencies Content: . Figure 7.4 Sample instance of relation r .  Page 312 Some functional dependencies are said to be trivial because they are satisfied by all relations. For example, A → A is satisfied by all relations involving attribute A . Reading the definition of functional dependency literally, we see that, for all tuples t 1 and t 2 such that t 1 [ A ] = t 2 [ A ], it is the case that t 1 [ A ] = t 2 [ A ]. Similarly, AB → A is satisfied by all relations involving attribute A . In general, a functional dependency of the form α → β is trivial if β ⊆ α. It is important to realize that an instance of a relation may satisfy some functional dependencies that are not required to hold on the relation's schema. In the instance of the classroom relation of Figure 7.5, we see that room\_number → capacity is satisfied. However, we believe that, in the real world, two classrooms in different buildings can have the same room number but with different room capacity. Thus, it is possible, at some time, to have an instance of the classroom relation in which room\_number → capacity is not satisfied. So, we would not include room\_number → capacity in the set of functional dependencies that hold on the schema for the classroom relation. However, we would expect the functional dependency building, room\_number → capacity to hold on the classroom schema. Figure 7.5 An instance of the classroom relation. Because we assume that attribute names have only one meaning in the database schema, if we state that a functional dependency α → β holds as a constraint on the database, then for any schema R such that α ⊆ R and β ⊆ R , α → β must hold.  Given that a set of functional dependencies F holds on a relation r ( R ), it may be possible to infer that certain other functional dependencies must also hold on the relation

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Content: A database models a set of entities and relationships in the real world. There are usually a variety of constraints (rules) on the data in the real world. For example, some of the constraints that are expected to hold in a university database are: 1. Students and instructors are uniquely identified by their ID. 2. Each student and instructor has only one name. 3. Each instructor and student is (primarily) associated with only one department. 2 4. Each department has only one value for its budget, and only one associated build-ing. Page 309 An instance of a relation that satisfies all such real-world constraints is called a legal instance of the relation; a legal instance of a database is one where all the relation instances are legal instances.

Description: Chapter 7 Section 7.2  Decomposition Using Functional Dependencies Section 7.2.2 Keys and Functional Dependencies Content: .  Given that a set of functional dependencies F holds on a relation r ( R ), it may be possible to infer that certain other functional dependencies must also hold on the relation. For example, given a schema r(A, B, C ), if functional dependencies A → B and B → C hold on r , we can infer the functional dependency A → C must also hold on r . This is because, given any value of A , there can be only one corresponding value for B , and for that value of B , there can only be one corresponding value for C . We study in Section 7.4.1, how to make such inferences. We shall use the notation F + to denote the closure of the set F , that is, the set of all functional dependencies that can be inferred given the set F . F + contains all of the functional dependencies in F .

The nested-loop join algorithm is a type of join algorithm that uses a nested loop structure to join two sorted tables. It works by iterating over the rows of the first table and matching them with the rows of the second table based on a common key.

Here's a step-by-step explanation of how the nested-loop join algorithm works:

1. The algorithm starts by iterating over the rows of the first table (the "left table") and storing them in an array or list.
2. For each row in the left table, it checks if the row matches any row in the second table (the "right table") based on a common key.
3. If a match is found, the algorithm stores the matching row in a temporary array or list.
4. The algorithm then iterates over the rows of the second table and checks if the row matches any row in the temporary array or list.
5. If a match is found, the algorithm stores the matching row in the final result.
6. The algorithm repeats steps 2-5 until all rows in the second table have been matched.

The nested-loop join algorithm has several advantages, including:

* It can handle large datasets efficiently, as it only requires iterating over the rows of the first table once.
* It can handle large datasets because it only requires storing the matching rows in memory.
* It can handle large datasets because it can use a temporary array or list to store the matching rows.

However, the nested-loop join algorithm also has some disadvantages, including:

* It can be slow for small datasets, as it requires iterating over the rows of the first table multiple times.
* It can be memory-intensive, as it requires storing the matching rows in memory.

Overall, the nested-loop join algorithm is a powerful and efficient join algorithm that can handle large datasets efficiently.

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.7  Evaluation of Expressions Section 15.7.2 Pipelining Section 15.7.2.2 Evaluation Algorithms for Pipelining Content: . Other operations, such as join, are not inherently blocking, but specific evaluation algorithms may be blocking. For example, the indexed nested loops join algorithm can output result tuples as it gets tuples for the outer relation. It is therefore pipelined on its outer (left-hand side) relation; however, it is blocking on its indexed (right-hand side) input, since the index must be fully constructed before the indexed nested-loop join algorithm can execute. The hash-join algorithm is a blocking operation on both inputs, since it requires both its inputs to be fully retrieved and partitioned before it outputs any tuples. How ever, hash-join partitions each of its inputs, and then performs multiple build-probe steps, once per partition. Thus, the hash-join algorithm has 3 steps: (i) partitioning of the first input, (ii) partitioning of the second input, and (iii) the build-probe step. The partitioning step for each input can accept tuples as they are generated by the input, and can thus be pipelined with its input. The build-probe step can output tuples to its consumer as the tuples are generated, and can thus be pipelined with its consumer. But the two partitioning steps are connected to the build-probe step by non-pipelined edges, since build-probe can start only after partitioning has been completed on both inputs. Hybrid hash join can be viewed as partially pipelined on the probe relation, since it can output tuples from the first partition as tuples are received for the probe relation. However, tuples that are not in the first partition will be output only after the entire pipelined input relation is received. Hybrid hash join thus provides fully pipelined evaluation on its probe input if the build input fits entirely in memory, or nearly pipelined evaluation if most of the build input fits in memory

The merge-join algorithm associates one pointer with each relation. These pointers point initially to the first tuple of the respective relations. As the algorithm proceeds, the pointers move through the relation. A group of tuples of one relation with the same value on the join attributes is read into S s . The algorithm in Figure 15.7 requires that every set of tuples S s fit in main memory; we discuss extensions of the algorithm to avoid this requirement shortly. If either of the input relations r and s is not sorted on the join attributes, they can be sorted first, and then the merge-join algorithm can be used. The merge-join algorithm can also be easily extended from natural joins to the more general case of equi-joins.

The nested-loop join algorithm is a simple and straightforward method for performing joins between two relations. Here's how it works:

1. **Initialization**: The algorithm starts by creating pointers to the first tuples in both relations (R and S).

2. **Pointer Movement**: As the algorithm progresses, these pointers move through their respective relations. This movement allows the algorithm to access tuples from both relations simultaneously.

3. **Matching**: For each tuple in relation R, the algorithm checks if there exists a matching tuple in relation S based on the join attributes. If a matching tuple is found, the algorithm concatenates the attributes of the matching tuples and projects out any repeated attributes.

4. **Output**: The resulting tuples are outputted as the join operation progresses.

5. **Blocking**: The nested-loop join algorithm is inherently blocking. This means that it cannot output result tuples until it has fully processed all tuples from relation R. Similarly, it cannot output result tuples until it has fully processed all tuples from relation S.

6. **Efficiency**: The efficiency of the nested-loop join algorithm depends on the size of the relations. If the relations are large, the algorithm can be very slow because it must process every tuple in both relations.

7. **Indexing**: If an index is available on one of the relations, the nested-loop join algorithm can be optimized. For example, if an index is available on relation S, the algorithm can use the index to quickly find matching tuples in relation S, potentially reducing the number of comparisons needed.

8. **Nested-Loop Join Algorithm**: The algorithm can be visualized as a nested loop where the outer loop iterates over tuples in relation R, and the inner loop iterates over tuples in relation S to find matching tuples.

The nested-loop join algorithm is expensive in terms of time complexity because it requires a single pass through both the build and probe inputs, which can be a significant amount of work, especially for large datasets. This is because the algorithm needs to scan through the entire build relation and probe relation to find matching records, which can be time-consuming.

To optimize the performance of the nested-loop join algorithm, several strategies can be employed:

1.  **Use a smaller input relation**: If possible, use the smaller input relation as the build relation. This can reduce the amount of work required by the algorithm.
2.  **Choose a larger hash index**: If the size of the build relation is b s blocks, choose a larger hash index (n h) that can accommodate all the tuples in the build relation. This can reduce the number of passes required by the algorithm.
3.  **Use a more efficient hash function**: Choose a hash function that is more efficient than the default one used by the algorithm. This can reduce the number of passes required by the algorithm.
4.  **Use a parallel join**: If the dataset is very large, consider using a parallel join algorithm, which can take advantage of multiple CPU cores to speed up the process.
5.  **Use a hybrid join algorithm**: Consider using a hybrid join algorithm that combines the benefits of nested-loop join with other join algorithms, such as hash join or merge join.
6.  **Optimize the join order**: Optimize the join order to reduce the number of passes required by the algorithm. For example, if the join order is optimized to use a different join algorithm for each partition of the build relation, this can reduce the number of passes required by the algorithm.
7.  **Use a more efficient data structure**: Consider using a more efficient data structure, such as a trie or a suffix tree, to store the build relation. This can reduce the amount of work required by the algorithm.

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.1 Cost-Based Join-Order Selection Content: . The join cost formulae that we saw in   Page 770 Chapter 15 can be used with appropriate modifications to ignore the cost of reading the input relations. Note that indexed nested loops join is treated differently from other join techniques: the plan as well as the cost are different in this case, since we do not perform a relation/index scan of the inner input, and the index lookup cost is included in the cost of indexed nested loops join. The procedure picks the cheapest plan from among all the alternatives for dividing S into two sets and the algorithms for joining the results of the two sets. The cheapest plan and its cost are stored in the array bestplan and returned by the procedure. The time complexity of the procedure can be shown to be O(3 n ) (see Practice Exercise 16.13). The order in which tuples are generated by the join of a set of relations is important for finding the best overall join order, since it can affect the cost of further joins. For instance, if merge join is used, a potentially expensive sort operation is required on the input, unless the input is already sorted on the join attribute. A particular sort order of the tuples is said to be an interesting sort order if it could be useful for a later operation. For instance, generating the result of r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 sorted on the attributes common with r 4 or r 5 may be useful, but generating it sorted on the attributes common to only r 1 and r 2 is not useful. Using merge join for computing r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 may be costlier than using some other join technique, but it may provide an output sorted in an interesting sort order. Hence, it is not sufficient to find the best join order for each subset of the set of n given relations. Instead, we have to find the best join order for each subset, for each interesting sort order of the join result for that subset

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.3 Heuristics in Optimization Content: . Contrast this cost with the O (3 n ) time required to find the best overall join order. The System R optimizer uses heuristics to push selections and projections down the query tree.  ## Figure 16.8 Left-deep join trees. A heuristic approach to reduce the cost of join-order selection, which was originally used in some versions of Oracle, works roughly this way: For an n -way join, it considers n evaluation plans. Each plan uses a left-deep join order, starting with a different one of the n relations. The heuristic constructs the join order for each of the n evaluation plans by repeatedly selecting the 'best' relation to join next, on the basis of a ranking of the available access paths. Either nested-loop or sort-merge join is chosen for each of the joins, depending on the available access paths. Finally, the heuristic chooses one of the n evaluation plans in a heuristic manner, on the basis of minimizing the number of nested-loop joins that do not have an index available on the inner relation and on the number of sortmerge joins. Query-optimization approaches that apply heuristic plan choices for some parts of the query, with cost-based choice based on generation of alternative access plans on other parts of the query, have been adopted in several systems. The approach used in System R and in its successor, the  Page 774 Starburst project, is a hierarchical procedure based on the nested-block concept of SQL. The cost-based optimization techniques described here are used for each block of the query separately. The optimizers in several database products, such as IBM DB2 and Oracle, are based on the above approach, with extensions to handle other operations such as aggregation. For compound SQL queries (using the ∪, ∩, or - operation), the optimizer processes each component separately and combines the evaluation plans to form the overall evaluation plan. Most optimizers allow a cost budget to be specified for query optimization

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.4 Optimizing Nested Subqueries Content: Page 775 SQL conceptually treats nested subqueries in the where clause as functions that take parameters and return either a single value or a set of values (possibly an empty set). The parameters are the variables from an outer-level query that are used in the nested subquery (these variables are called correlation variables ). For instance, suppose we have the following query, to find the names of all instructors who taught a course in 2019:  Conceptually, the subquery can be viewed as a function that takes a parameter (here, instructor . ID ) and returns the set of all courses taught in 2019 by instructors (with the same ID ). SQL evaluates the overall query (conceptually) by computing the Cartesian product of the relations in the outer from clause and then testing the predicates in the where clause for each tuple in the product. In the preceding example, the predicate tests if the result of the subquery evaluation is empty. In practice, the predicates in the where clause that can be used as join predicates, or as selection predicates are evaluated as part of the selections on relations or to perform joins that avoid Cartesian products. Predicates involving nested subqueries in the where clause are evaluated subsequently, since they are usually expensive, by invoking the subquery as a function. The technique of evaluating a nested subquery by invoking it as a function is called correlated evaluation . Correlated evaluation is not very efficient, since the subquery is separately evaluated for each tuple in the outer level query. A large number of random disk I/O operations may result. SQL optimizers therefore attempt to transform nested subqueries into joins, where possible. Efficient join algorithms help avoid expensive random I/O. Where the transformation is not possible, the optimizer keeps the subqueries as separate expressions, optimizes them separately, and then evaluates them by correlated evaluation

More precisely stated, we have to account for the extra space occupied by the hash index on the partition as well, so n h should be correspondingly larger. For simplicity, we sometimes ignore the space requirement of the hash index in our analysis.

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.1 Cost-Based Join-Order Selection Content: . The join cost formulae that we saw in   Page 770 Chapter 15 can be used with appropriate modifications to ignore the cost of reading the input relations. Note that indexed nested loops join is treated differently from other join techniques: the plan as well as the cost are different in this case, since we do not perform a relation/index scan of the inner input, and the index lookup cost is included in the cost of indexed nested loops join. The procedure picks the cheapest plan from among all the alternatives for dividing S into two sets and the algorithms for joining the results of the two sets. The cheapest plan and its cost are stored in the array bestplan and returned by the procedure. The time complexity of the procedure can be shown to be O(3 n ) (see Practice Exercise 16.13). The order in which tuples are generated by the join of a set of relations is important for finding the best overall join order, since it can affect the cost of further joins. For instance, if merge join is used, a potentially expensive sort operation is required on the input, unless the input is already sorted on the join attribute. A particular sort order of the tuples is said to be an interesting sort order if it could be useful for a later operation. For instance, generating the result of r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 sorted on the attributes common with r 4 or r 5 may be useful, but generating it sorted on the attributes common to only r 1 and r 2 is not useful. Using merge join for computing r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 may be costlier than using some other join technique, but it may provide an output sorted in an interesting sort order. Hence, it is not sufficient to find the best join order for each subset of the set of n given relations. Instead, we have to find the best join order for each subset, for each interesting sort order of the join result for that subset

The nested-loop join algorithm can be expensive in terms of time complexity because it requires a full scan of both input relations to find matching tuples. This can result in a high number of comparisons and potentially large disk I/O operations, especially when dealing with large datasets. The time complexity of nested-loop join is O(n * m), where n and m are the number of tuples in the two relations being joined.

To optimize the performance of nested-loop joins, several strategies can be employed:

1. **Use Indexes**: If the join key is indexed, the system can use the index to quickly find matching tuples in the probe relation. This can significantly reduce the number of comparisons and improve performance.

2. **Index Nested-Loop Join**: This is a variant of nested-loop join where the index on the join key is used to retrieve records from the probe relation. This can be more efficient than a full scan of the probe relation, especially if the index is well-organized and the join key is frequently used.

3. **Dynamic Choice of Join Algorithm**: Some systems allow dynamic selection of join algorithms based on runtime information. For example, if the number of tuples in the outer relation is known, a hash join might be more efficient than a nested-loop join.

4. **Join Order Optimization**: Choosing the best join order can also improve performance. The optimal join order depends on the data distribution and the join attributes. Algorithms like the one described in Chapter 16, which uses heuristics to select the best join order, can help in this regard.

5. **Correlated Subquery Optimization**: For nested subqueries in the WHERE clause, SQL optimizers can transform them into joins or use correlated evaluation to avoid expensive repeated evaluations.

6. **Partitioning**: If the data is partitioned, the optimizer can choose the best partitioning strategy to minimize the number of comparisons and I/O operations.

7. **Memory Management**: Efficient memory management, such as using smaller relations as the build relation

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.7  Evaluation of Expressions Section 15.7.2 Pipelining Section 15.7.2.2 Evaluation Algorithms for Pipelining Content: . Other operations, such as join, are not inherently blocking, but specific evaluation algorithms may be blocking. For example, the indexed nested loops join algorithm can output result tuples as it gets tuples for the outer relation. It is therefore pipelined on its outer (left-hand side) relation; however, it is blocking on its indexed (right-hand side) input, since the index must be fully constructed before the indexed nested-loop join algorithm can execute. The hash-join algorithm is a blocking operation on both inputs, since it requires both its inputs to be fully retrieved and partitioned before it outputs any tuples. How ever, hash-join partitions each of its inputs, and then performs multiple build-probe steps, once per partition. Thus, the hash-join algorithm has 3 steps: (i) partitioning of the first input, (ii) partitioning of the second input, and (iii) the build-probe step. The partitioning step for each input can accept tuples as they are generated by the input, and can thus be pipelined with its input. The build-probe step can output tuples to its consumer as the tuples are generated, and can thus be pipelined with its consumer. But the two partitioning steps are connected to the build-probe step by non-pipelined edges, since build-probe can start only after partitioning has been completed on both inputs. Hybrid hash join can be viewed as partially pipelined on the probe relation, since it can output tuples from the first partition as tuples are received for the probe relation. However, tuples that are not in the first partition will be output only after the entire pipelined input relation is received. Hybrid hash join thus provides fully pipelined evaluation on its probe input if the build input fits entirely in memory, or nearly pipelined evaluation if most of the build input fits in memory

Contrast this cost with the O (3 n ) time required to find the best overall join order. The System R optimizer uses heuristics to push selections and projections down the query tree.  ## Figure 16.8 Left-deep join trees. A heuristic approach to reduce the cost of join-order selection, which was originally used in some versions of Oracle, works roughly this way: For an n -way join, it considers n evaluation plans. Each plan uses a left-deep join order, starting with a different one of the n relations. The heuristic constructs the join order for each of the n evaluation plans by repeatedly selecting the 'best' relation to join next, on the basis of a ranking of the available access paths. Either nested-loop or sort-merge join is chosen for each of the joins, depending on the available access paths. Finally, the heuristic chooses one of the n evaluation plans in a heuristic manner, on the basis of minimizing the number of nested-loop joins that do not have an index available on the inner relation and on the number of sortmerge joins. Query-optimization approaches that apply heuristic plan choices for some parts of the query, with cost-based choice based on generation of alternative access plans on other parts of the query, have been adopted in several systems. The approach used in System R and in its successor, the  Page 774 Starburst project, is a hierarchical procedure based on the nested-block concept of SQL. The cost-based optimization techniques described here are used for each block of the query separately. The optimizers in several database products, such as IBM DB2 and Oracle, are based on the above approach, with extensions to handle other operations such as aggregation. For compound SQL queries (using the ∪, ∩, or - operation), the optimizer processes each component separately and combines the evaluation plans to form the overall evaluation plan. Most optimizers allow a cost budget to be specified for query optimization

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.5  Join Operation Section 15.5.5 Hash Join Section 15.5.5.4 Cost of Hash Join Content: . However, in case a secondary index is used, and the number of tuples in the outer relation is large, indexed nested loops join can have a very high cost, as compared to hash join. If the number of tuples in the outer relation is known at query optimization time, the best join algorithm can be chosen at that time. However, in some cases, for example, when there is a selection condition on the outer input, the optimizer makes a decision based on an estimate that may potentially be imprecise. The number of tuples in the outer relation may be found only at runtime, for example, after executing selection. Some systems allow a dynamic choice between the two algorithms at run time, after finding the number of tuples in the outer input.

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.7  Evaluation of Expressions Section 15.7.2 Pipelining Section 15.7.2.2 Evaluation Algorithms for Pipelining Content: . Other operations, such as join, are not inherently blocking, but specific evaluation algorithms may be blocking. For example, the indexed nested loops join algorithm can output result tuples as it gets tuples for the outer relation. It is therefore pipelined on its outer (left-hand side) relation; however, it is blocking on its indexed (right-hand side) input, since the index must be fully constructed before the indexed nested-loop join algorithm can execute. The hash-join algorithm is a blocking operation on both inputs, since it requires both its inputs to be fully retrieved and partitioned before it outputs any tuples. How ever, hash-join partitions each of its inputs, and then performs multiple build-probe steps, once per partition. Thus, the hash-join algorithm has 3 steps: (i) partitioning of the first input, (ii) partitioning of the second input, and (iii) the build-probe step. The partitioning step for each input can accept tuples as they are generated by the input, and can thus be pipelined with its input. The build-probe step can output tuples to its consumer as the tuples are generated, and can thus be pipelined with its consumer. But the two partitioning steps are connected to the build-probe step by non-pipelined edges, since build-probe can start only after partitioning has been completed on both inputs. Hybrid hash join can be viewed as partially pipelined on the probe relation, since it can output tuples from the first partition as tuples are received for the probe relation. However, tuples that are not in the first partition will be output only after the entire pipelined input relation is received. Hybrid hash join thus provides fully pipelined evaluation on its probe input if the build input fits entirely in memory, or nearly pipelined evaluation if most of the build input fits in memory

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.5  Join Operation Section 15.5.5 Hash Join Section 15.5.5.1 Basics Content: . In the course of the indexed nested-loop  Page 714 join, the system uses this hash index to retrieve records that match records in the probe input. The build and probe phases require only a single pass through both the build and probe inputs. It is straightforward to extend the hash-join algorithm to compute general equi-joins. The value n h must be chosen to be large enough such that, for each i , the tuples in the partition s i of the build relation, along with the hash index on the partition, fit in memory. It is not necessary for the partitions of the probe relation to fit in memory. It is best to use the smaller input relation as the build relation. If the size of the build relation is b s blocks, then, for each of the n h partitions to be of size less than or equal to M , n h must be at least ⌈ b s /M ⌉. More precisely stated, we have to account for the extra space occupied by the hash index on the partition as well, so n h should be correspondingly larger. For simplicity, we sometimes ignore the space requirement of the hash index in our analysis.

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.1 Cost-Based Join-Order Selection Content: . The join cost formulae that we saw in   Page 770 Chapter 15 can be used with appropriate modifications to ignore the cost of reading the input relations. Note that indexed nested loops join is treated differently from other join techniques: the plan as well as the cost are different in this case, since we do not perform a relation/index scan of the inner input, and the index lookup cost is included in the cost of indexed nested loops join. The procedure picks the cheapest plan from among all the alternatives for dividing S into two sets and the algorithms for joining the results of the two sets. The cheapest plan and its cost are stored in the array bestplan and returned by the procedure. The time complexity of the procedure can be shown to be O(3 n ) (see Practice Exercise 16.13). The order in which tuples are generated by the join of a set of relations is important for finding the best overall join order, since it can affect the cost of further joins. For instance, if merge join is used, a potentially expensive sort operation is required on the input, unless the input is already sorted on the join attribute. A particular sort order of the tuples is said to be an interesting sort order if it could be useful for a later operation. For instance, generating the result of r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 sorted on the attributes common with r 4 or r 5 may be useful, but generating it sorted on the attributes common to only r 1 and r 2 is not useful. Using merge join for computing r 1 ⋈ r 2 ⋈ r 3 may be costlier than using some other join technique, but it may provide an output sorted in an interesting sort order. Hence, it is not sufficient to find the best join order for each subset of the set of n given relations. Instead, we have to find the best join order for each subset, for each interesting sort order of the join result for that subset

To compare and contrast the block nested-loop join with the basic nested-loop join, let's first define each and then discuss their advantages and disadvantages.

### Basic Nested-Loop Join
- **Definition**: In a basic nested-loop join, the system retrieves tuples from the outer relation and then matches them with tuples from the inner relation based on the join condition. This process is repeated for each tuple in the outer relation.
- **Advantages**:
  - **Simplicity**: The basic nested-loop join is straightforward and easy to implement.
  - **Flexibility**: It can handle any join condition without requiring additional optimizations.
- **Disadvantages**:
  - **Blocking**: The join operation is inherently blocking, meaning it blocks on the outer relation until all tuples from the inner relation are matched.
  - **Cost**: The cost of the join operation is high, especially when the outer relation is large and the inner relation is small, leading to a high number of tuple comparisons.

### Block Nested-Loop Join
- **Definition**: In a block nested-loop join, the system retrieves tuples from the outer relation and then matches them with tuples from the inner relation in blocks. Each block of the outer relation is joined with the inner relation separately.
- **Advantages**:
  - **Parallelism**: By processing blocks of the outer relation in parallel, the join operation can be more efficient, especially when the outer relation is large.
  - **Reduced Blocking**: The join operation is less blocking since it can process blocks of the outer relation independently.
- **Disadvantages**:
  - **Complexity**: Implementing block nested-loop join requires additional logic to manage the blocks and ensure that the join operation is correctly performed.
  - **Additional Cost**: The overhead of managing blocks and ensuring correct join can add to the overall cost, especially if not implemented efficiently.

### Comparison
- **Blocking**: The basic nested-loop join is inherently blocking, whereas the block nested-loop join is

The parameters are the variables from an outer-level query that are used in the nested subquery (these variables are called correlation variables ). For instance, suppose we have the following query, to find the names of all instructors who taught a course in 2019:  Conceptually, the subquery can be viewed as a function that takes a parameter (here, instructor . ID ) and returns the set of all courses taught in 2019 by instructors (with the same ID ). SQL evaluates the overall query (conceptually) by computing the Cartesian product of the relations in the outer from clause and then testing the predicates in the where clause for each tuple in the product. In the preceding example, the predicate tests if the result of the subquery evaluation is empty. In practice, the predicates in the where clause that can be used as join predicates, or as selection predicates are evaluated as part of the selections on relations or to perform joins that avoid Cartesian products. Predicates involving nested subqueries in the where clause are evaluated subsequently, since they are usually expensive, by invoking the subquery as a function. The technique of evaluating a nested subquery by invoking it as a function is called correlated evaluation . Correlated evaluation is not very efficient, since the subquery is separately evaluated for each tuple in the outer level query. A large number of random disk I/O operations may result. SQL optimizers therefore attempt to transform nested subqueries into joins, where possible. Efficient join algorithms help avoid expensive random I/O. Where the transformation is not possible, the optimizer keeps the subqueries as separate expressions, optimizes them separately, and then evaluates them by correlated evaluation

As an attempt at transforming a nested subquery into a join, the query in the preceding example can be rewritten in relational algebra as a join: Page 776 Unfortunately, the above query is not quite correct, since the multiset versions of the relational algebra operators are used in SQL implementations, and as a result an instructor who teaches multiple sections in 2019 will appear multiple times in the result of the relational algebra query, although that instructor would appear only once in the SQL query result. Using the set version of the relational algebra operators will not help either, since if there are two instructors with the same name who teach in 2019, the name would appear only once with the set version of relational algebra, but would appear twice in the SQL query result. (We note that the set version of relational algebra would give the correct result if the query output contained the primary key of instructor, namely ID .) To properly reflect SQL semantics, the number of duplicates of a tuple in the result should not change because of the rewriting. The semijoin operator of the relational algebra provides a solution to this problem. The multiset version of the semijoin operator r ⋉ θ s is defined as follows: if a tuple r i appears n times in r , it appears n times in the result of r ⋉θ if there is at least one tuple s j such that r i and s j together satisfy predicate θ; otherwise r i does not appear in the result. The set version of the semijoin operator r ⋉ θ s can be defined as Π R ( r ⋈ θ s ), where R is the set of attributes in the schema of r . The multiset version of the semijoin operator outputs the same tuples, but the number of duplicates of each tuple r i in the semijoin result is the same as the number of duplicates of r i in r

To optimize the query performance for retrieving all students who are enrolled in a specific course, we can apply the concepts learned from the nested-loop join algorithm in the following steps:

1. **Identify the Course**: First, we need to identify the specific course for which we want to retrieve students. This can be done by querying the database for the course ID.

2. **Use Nested-Loop Join**: Next, we use the nested-loop join algorithm to retrieve all students who are enrolled in the specified course. This involves creating a nested loop query that iterates over the students and their enrollment status.

3. **Filter Students**: After retrieving the students, we filter them based on the course enrollment status. This can be done by adding a WHERE clause that filters the students based on the course ID.

4. **Optimize the Query**: Finally, we optimize the query to retrieve the filtered students efficiently. This involves using correlated evaluation to evaluate the nested loop query and the WHERE clause for each tuple in the product.

Here is a step-by-step implementation of the above steps:

```sql
-- Step 1: Identify the course
SELECT course_id
FROM courses
WHERE course_name = 'specific course';

-- Step 2: Use nested-loop join
WITH students AS (
    SELECT student_id, course_id
    FROM students
    WHERE course_id = 'specific course'
)
SELECT s.student_id
FROM students s
JOIN courses c ON s.course_id = c.course_id
WHERE c.course_id = 'specific course';

-- Step 3: Filter students
WITH filtered_students AS (
    SELECT s.student_id
    FROM students s
    JOIN courses c ON s.course_id = c.course_id
    WHERE c.course_id = 'specific course'
)
SELECT student_id
FROM filtered_students
WHERE student_id IN (
    SELECT student_id
    FROM courses
    WHERE course_id = 'specific course'
);
```

As an attempt at transforming a nested subquery into a join, the query in the preceding example can be rewritten in relational algebra as a join: Page 776 Unfortunately, the above query is not quite correct, since the multiset versions of the relational algebra operators are used in SQL implementations, and as a result an instructor who teaches multiple sections in 2019 will appear multiple times in the result of the relational algebra query, although that instructor would appear only once in the SQL query result. Using the set version of the relational algebra operators will not help either, since if there are two instructors with the same name who teach in 2019, the name would appear only once with the set version of relational algebra, but would appear twice in the SQL query result. (We note that the set version of relational algebra would give the correct result if the query output contained the primary key of instructor, namely ID .) To properly reflect SQL semantics, the number of duplicates of a tuple in the result should not change because of the rewriting. The semijoin operator of the relational algebra provides a solution to this problem. The multiset version of the semijoin operator r ⋉ θ s is defined as follows: if a tuple r i appears n times in r , it appears n times in the result of r ⋉θ if there is at least one tuple s j such that r i and s j together satisfy predicate θ; otherwise r i does not appear in the result. The set version of the semijoin operator r ⋉ θ s can be defined as Π R ( r ⋈ θ s ), where R is the set of attributes in the schema of r . The multiset version of the semijoin operator outputs the same tuples, but the number of duplicates of each tuple r i in the semijoin result is the same as the number of duplicates of r i in r

Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.3 Heuristics in Optimization Content: . Contrast this cost with the O (3 n ) time required to find the best overall join order. The System R optimizer uses heuristics to push selections and projections down the query tree.  ## Figure 16.8 Left-deep join trees. A heuristic approach to reduce the cost of join-order selection, which was originally used in some versions of Oracle, works roughly this way: For an n -way join, it considers n evaluation plans. Each plan uses a left-deep join order, starting with a different one of the n relations. The heuristic constructs the join order for each of the n evaluation plans by repeatedly selecting the 'best' relation to join next, on the basis of a ranking of the available access paths. Either nested-loop or sort-merge join is chosen for each of the joins, depending on the available access paths. Finally, the heuristic chooses one of the n evaluation plans in a heuristic manner, on the basis of minimizing the number of nested-loop joins that do not have an index available on the inner relation and on the number of sortmerge joins. Query-optimization approaches that apply heuristic plan choices for some parts of the query, with cost-based choice based on generation of alternative access plans on other parts of the query, have been adopted in several systems. The approach used in System R and in its successor, the  Page 774 Starburst project, is a hierarchical procedure based on the nested-block concept of SQL. The cost-based optimization techniques described here are used for each block of the query separately. The optimizers in several database products, such as IBM DB2 and Oracle, are based on the above approach, with extensions to handle other operations such as aggregation. For compound SQL queries (using the ∪, ∩, or - operation), the optimizer processes each component separately and combines the evaluation plans to form the overall evaluation plan. Most optimizers allow a cost budget to be specified for query optimization

To optimize the query performance for retrieving all students who are enrolled in a specific course using the nested-loop join algorithm, we can follow these steps:

1. **Understand the Query**: The query is to find all students enrolled in a specific course. Let's denote the student relation as `S` and the course relation as `C`. The specific course we are interested in is `C_id`.

2. **Relational Algebra Representation**: The relational algebra representation of the query is:
   \[
   \pi_{\text{S_ID, S_Name}}(\text{S} \times \text{C})
   \]
   This means we are projecting the student ID and name from the Cartesian product of the student and course relations.

3. **Nested-Loop Join Algorithm**: The nested-loop join algorithm involves two passes through the relations. In the first pass, we read all tuples from the student relation `S`. In the second pass, we read all tuples from the course relation `C` and check if each course tuple matches any student tuple.

4. **Optimization Techniques**:
   - **Hash Join**: If the course relation is large and the student relation is small, a hash join can be more efficient. We can hash the student relation and probe it with the course relation.
   - **Correlated Subqueries**: If the course relation is large and the student relation is small, a correlated subquery can be used to avoid the Cartesian product. The subquery can be evaluated for each student tuple, reducing the number of tuples to be checked.
   - **Semijoin**: If the course relation is large and the student relation is small, a semijoin can be used to avoid duplicates. The semijoin operator ensures that each student tuple appears only once in the result.

chunks: Description: Chapter 16 Section 16.4  Choice of Evaluation Plans Section 16.4.4 Optimizing Nested Subqueries Content: Page 775 SQL conceptually treats nested subqueries in the where clause as functions that take parameters and return either a single value or a set of values (possibly an empty set). The parameters are the variables from an outer-level query that are used in the nested subquery (these variables are called correlation variables ). For instance, suppose we have the following query, to find the names of all instructors who taught a course in 2019:  Conceptually, the subquery can be viewed as a function that takes a parameter (here, instructor . ID ) and returns the set of all courses taught in 2019 by instructors (with the same ID ). SQL evaluates the overall query (conceptually) by computing the Cartesian product of the relations in the outer from clause and then testing the predicates in the where clause for each tuple in the product. In the preceding example, the predicate tests if the result of the subquery evaluation is empty. In practice, the predicates in the where clause that can be used as join predicates, or as selection predicates are evaluated as part of the selections on relations or to perform joins that avoid Cartesian products. Predicates involving nested subqueries in the where clause are evaluated subsequently, since they are usually expensive, by invoking the subquery as a function. The technique of evaluating a nested subquery by invoking it as a function is called correlated evaluation . Correlated evaluation is not very efficient, since the subquery is separately evaluated for each tuple in the outer level query. A large number of random disk I/O operations may result. SQL optimizers therefore attempt to transform nested subqueries into joins, where possible. Efficient join algorithms help avoid expensive random I/O. Where the transformation is not possible, the optimizer keeps the subqueries as separate expressions, optimizes them separately, and then evaluates them by correlated evaluation

Description: Chapter 15 Section 15.5  Join Operation Section 15.5.5 Hash Join Section 15.5.5.1 Basics Content: . In the course of the indexed nested-loop  Page 714 join, the system uses this hash index to retrieve records that match records in the probe input. The build and probe phases require only a single pass through both the build and probe inputs. It is straightforward to extend the hash-join algorithm to compute general equi-joins. The value n h must be chosen to be large enough such that, for each i , the tuples in the partition s i of the build relation, along with the hash index on the partition, fit in memory. It is not necessary for the partitions of the probe relation to fit in memory. It is best to use the smaller input relation as the build relation. If the size of the build relation is b s blocks, then, for each of the n h partitions to be of size less than or equal to M , n h must be at least ⌈ b s /M ⌉. More precisely stated, we have to account for the extra space occupied by the hash index on the partition as well, so n h should be correspondingly larger. For simplicity, we sometimes ignore the space requirement of the hash index in our analysis.

However, in case a secondary index is used, and the number of tuples in the outer relation is large, indexed nested loops join can have a very high cost, as compared to hash join. If the number of tuples in the outer relation is known at query optimization time, the best join algorithm can be chosen at that time. However, in some cases, for example, when there is a selection condition on the outer input, the optimizer makes a decision based on an estimate that may potentially be imprecise. The number of tuples in the outer relation may be found only at runtime, for example, after executing selection. Some systems allow a dynamic choice between the two algorithms at run time, after finding the number of tuples in the outer input.
